heading: In Home Caregiver Program
description: The Canadian government provides a remedy to Canadian families to hire full-time caregivers to provide care to their children, seniors, or persons with certified medical needs. You can use this opportunity to seek work permit and permanent residency in Canada.
benefitsHeading: Benefits of In Home Caregiver Program
b1: The employers can hire a full-time dedicated caregiver for the person in need, thereby creating a less stressful environment when caring for individuals with high needs.
b2: The work permit provides you with an opportunity to gain qualifying experience in Canada to apply for permanent residence.
b3: These permanent residence applications do not have any points system like Express Entry or provincial nomination programs. It is based on a first-come, first-served basis.
b4: The language requirements for both work permit and permanent residency application are very low.
b5: The spouse and dependent children may accompany you throughout the work permit duration depending on your wage rate and National Occupational Classification (NOC) level.
eligibilityHeading: Eligibility Criteria for In Home Caregiver Program
eligibilityDescription: In home Caregiver program is a two-step process:
elibilityS1: Step 1: Your employer applies for Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA).
elibilityS2: Step 2: You apply for work permit.
s1Heading: Step 1: Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA) Process
s1Desc: For the employer considering hiring a foreign worker on a caregiver LMIA, here is the eligibility check:
s1l1: Have a valid payroll account with Canada Revenue Agency (CRA).
s1l2: Provide proof of individual requiring care such as a long form birth certificate, passport or physician’s note, depending on the type of care required.
s1l3: Must demonstrate their financial ability to pay the caregiver’s wages.
s1l4: Pay the foreign worker the prevailing wage for the position.
s1discalmier: Your employer is required to apply for a Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA). The application is submitted online by the employer on LMIA portal.
foreignHeading: The employers in Canada can hire a foreign worker for one of the two categories:
foreign1Desc: This category includes positions such as childcare provider, live-in caregiver, nanny that fall under NOC 44100. The children to whom care will be provided should be under 18 years of age.
foreign2: 2. Caregivers for people with high medical needs
foreign2Desc: This category includes workers who provide care to people with the age of 65 years or over, people with disabilities, a chronic or terminal illness. The positions included are as follows:
f2l1: Registered nurse or registered psychiatric nurse (NOC 31301)
f2l3: Attendant for persons with disabilities, home support worker, live-in caregiver, personal care attendant (NOC 44101)
proofDesc1: At the time of LMIA application, the employer is required to provide proof to Service Canada to prove that they or their dependant is in need of care by a full-time caregiver.
proofDesc2: For age and parentage for each child under the age of 18, they can provide one of the following documents:
under18l4: Medical doctor's note confirming the pregnancy and the due date
seniorHeading: For age for each senior, 65 years or older, they can provide one of the following documents:
disabilityHeading: For disability, chronic or terminal illness for each disabled, chronically or terminally ill person, they can provide:
disabilityl1: Completed Schedule H - Medical disability, chronic or terminal illness certificate (EMP5600) form, signed and dated by the physician, or
disabilityl2: Physician's note attesting that the patient has a disability, chronic or terminal illness and that they require access to a live-in caregiver
step2Heading: Step 2: You Apply for Work Permit
step2Desc: If you are a foreign national residing within Canada on a work permit or study permit, you can apply for a work permit under Temporary Foreign Worker Program after your employer obtains a positive Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA). You cannot apply for a work permit with caregiver LMIA if you are not currently already authorized to work or study (whether you are physically within or outside Canada). You must have a valid passport, be fluent in English or French and meet the minimum education and experience requirements for the job.
applyHeading: How to Apply for In Home Caregiver Program?
emp1: Have an initial assessment performed by us to verify if the employer is eligible to apply for LMIA.
emp2: We will provide you checklist of documents and information required to start the initial process.
emp3: Perform recruitment efforts as per ESDC program requirements for each LMIA type – high wage or low wage occupation LMIA. (Hyperlink)
emp5: Prepare and submit LMIA application to Service Canada ESDS department.
emp6: Pay the fees when requested, within 2 business days.
emp7l1: If applying for LMIA to provide care for an individual requiring assistance with medical needs, the LMIA processing fee of $1000.00 is not required. A medical certificate attesting to the individual’s incapacity to care for themselves will have to be provided.
emp7l2: If applying for LMIA to provide care for a child under 13 years of age and the employer’s gross annual income is $150,000 or less, the LMIA processing fee of $1000.00 is not required.
emp8: Wait for LMIA processing time, which can range from 30 to 90 business days. If the Service Canada officer has any questions or concerns or requires any further information, the employer might be contacted for an interview.
emp9: You will receive a positive LMIA by email or in the inbox section of LMIA portal.
asF1: Apply for a work permit as per guidelines set by IRCC.
asF2: IRCC will assess your application to check if you meet the requirements.
asF3: If your application is approved, IRCC will issue you a work permit.
asF4: Begin working with your employer to gain qualifying work experience for permanent residency pathways.
s1: If you have received a refusal for any of the reasons mentioned above, do not worry. With over a decade of experience, we specialize in previously refused cases. While we don't provide jobs for LMIA, we can certainly assist you if you have a job offer. We have obtained approvals for clients who had multiple previous refusals. We achieve this with a tailored approach to your specific case, addressing each concern that the officer has listed in previous refusals. We use case law and find similar cases to your circumstances that had positive results, and we use them as precedents in cases we work on. This is why we have a high success rate.
s2: At Brightlight Immigration, we have a dedicated team of visa application specialists who can assist you from the start of the application process to obtaining your visa. Start your process now.
whuChooseDescription: Discover why Brightlight Immigration is the right choice for your immigration needs. Our experienced team offers personalized services to ensure the best possible outcome for your application.
wcu1: Experienced Team: Over a decade of experience in handling Immigration applications with a high success rate.
wcu2: Tailored Approach: Personalized services and strategies based on your specific case.
wcu3: High Success Rate: Proven track record using case law and precedents for positive results.
wcu4: Comprehensive Support: Assistance from the start of the application process to obtaining your PR.
q1: Can I get a caregiver job offer from my relative?
qa1: Yes, a family member or a relative can offer you the caregiver job. Being related to your employer does not make you ineligible to apply. IRCC will review all circumstances of the application to ensure eligibility, genuineness of the job offer and employer/employee relationship.
q2: Can I offer caregiver job to my relative?
qa2: Yes, you can hire a relative for the position of caregiver after obtaining a positive Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA).
q3: I am an employer. How long do I have to advertise for the position of caregiver?
qa3: You will have to advertise for 4 weeks as per temporary foreign worker guidelines for low-wage/high-wage occupation.
q4: How much does caregiver LMIA cost?
qa4:
The application processing fees is $1000.00. There are exemptions to paying this fee as follows:
  • If applying for LMIA to provide care for an individual requiring assistance with medical needs, the LMIA processing fee of $1000.00 is not required. A medical certificate attesting to the individual’s incapacity to care for themselves will have to be provided.
  • If applying for LMIA to provide care for a child under 13 years of age and the employer’s gross annual income is $150,000 or less, the LMIA processing fee of $1000.00 is not required.
q5: I am an employer. For an LMIA application, is it acceptable if the person requiring care live at a different address than me?
qa5: Yes, definitely. If you and the care recipient reside at different addresses, you will required to provide proof of the care recipient's address to ESDC, such as a driver's license, a utility bill, a provincial/territorial identification card, bank statement, notice of assessment from the CRA, or a statement of other government benefits, such as the Canada Pension Plan, Old Age Security or the Guaranteed Income Supplement. However, if you reside at the same address as the care recipient, you don't need to provide proof of address.
q6: I want to hire a caregiver for child or elderly parents in the private household, do I need to register with the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA)?
qa6: Yes, you are required to register with the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) and obtain a payroll account number for the purpose of creating an employer file with Job Bank and payroll of the caregiver.
q7: I am a visitor in Canada, can I obtain caregiver work permit after asking for Labour Market Impact Assessment from my employer?
qa7: No, this program has been closed and IRCC refuses to process the applications made by visitor for work permit under the Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP).
q8: Do I have to live in the residence of the employer?
qa8: No, it is not mandatory to live in the residence of the employer or where care to the individual will be provided.
